seo.title: Trang Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Trang Province travel guide: Emerald Cave, Koh Kradan, Koh Mook, Hat Chao Mai National Park, dugongs at Koh Libong. Area: 4,917 km², 10 districts. Plan your visit.

overview: Trang Province is one of southern Thailand's most rewarding off-the-beaten-track destinations — a place where limestone island chains meet dugong-rich seagrass beds and where the morning air smells of roast pork and steamer baskets. Situated on the Andaman coast roughly 830 km south of Bangkok, Trang is famous for its Andaman island archipelago (Koh Kradan, Koh Mook, and Koh Ngai), the otherworldly Emerald Cave (Tham Morakot), and Hat Chao Mai National Park. The provincial capital, Trang city, is a laid-back market town shaped by centuries of Hokkien Chinese immigration and an intense pride in its food culture — dim sum breakfasts and moo yang roast pork are near-religious rituals here. With 10 districts, 199 km of Andaman coastline, and far fewer tourists than neighboring Krabi or Koh Lanta, Trang rewards travelers who look just a little further.
geography: Trang covers 4,917.5 km² of the narrow Malay Peninsula, bordered by the Andaman Sea to the west and interior mountains to the east. The coastline is dotted with limestone karst islands protected within Hat Chao Mai National Park and the Libong Archipelago Non-Hunting Area. Trang's largest island, Koh Libong, sits 30 minutes by long-tail boat from the mainland and harbors over 12,000 rai of seagrass meadows — the primary food source for Thailand's largest remaining population of dugongs. The province shares geography with Krabi to the north, Nakhon Si Thammarat and Phatthalung to the east, and Satun to the south.
highlights: Emerald Cave (Tham Morakot) on Koh Mook — swim 80 meters through a dark tunnel to a hidden lagoon ringed by jungle cliffs
Koh Kradan — repeatedly voted Thailand's most beautiful island, with shallow turquoise reefs ideal for snorkeling directly from the beach
Hat Chao Mai National Park — 231 km² of protected coastline, mangroves, and offshore islands including Koh Mook, Koh Kradan, and Koh Cheuk
Koh Libong Dugong Conservation Zone — the only place in Thailand to see dugongs in their natural seagrass habitat
Trang dim sum and moo yang breakfast culture — a Hokkien Chinese legacy unique to this province
Koh Ngai (Koh Hai) — pristine coral reefs and long sandy beaches in an administratively Trang, experientially Andaman island
Trang town night market and clock tower area — authentic southern Thai urban life with zero tourist infrastructure
Rubber and palm oil plantation hinterland with limestone caves and jungle waterfalls

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Apr: The Andaman dry season brings calm seas, clear visibility, and the best snorkeling and island-hopping conditions. The Emerald Cave is only accessible during this period. Expect warm temperatures of 28–34°C and sunshine most days.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Oct and May: Transitional months with increasingly choppy seas. Some boat services to the outer islands (especially Koh Kradan) begin suspending operations in May. October can bring heavy rain but islands may still be reachable.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Sep: Southwest monsoon closes most island boat services and the Emerald Cave. Trang city and inland attractions remain accessible. Hotel rates drop significantly. The mainland food scene and rubber plantation country are enjoyable year-round.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November through April for the full island experience, including the Emerald Cave and Koh Kradan. December to February offers the best sea conditions. Trang city is worth visiting any time of year for its unique food culture alone.

topAttractions.name: Emerald Cave (Tham Morakot)
topAttractions.description: Koh Mook's show-stopping attraction: an 80-meter sea cave that must be swum through in near-total darkness to reach a hidden inland lagoon — a collapsed doline surrounded by sheer jungle-covered limestone cliffs with a white sand beach at the bottom. The cave is only accessible by boat and only during the dry season (Nov–Apr). Most visitors join half-day tours from Trang or Koh Lanta piers.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB national park fee per person (included in most organized tours). Cave access by longtail boat: approx. 400 THB per boat for small groups

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Hat Chao Mai National Park

topAttractions.name: Koh Kradan
topAttractions.description: Widely regarded as one of Thailand's most beautiful islands, Koh Kradan sits within Hat Chao Mai National Park and features a long white-sand west-facing beach with coral reefs accessible directly from shore — no boat needed to snorkel among reef fish and sea turtles. The island is nearly uninhabited beyond a handful of eco-resorts and the national park ranger station. Voted best beach in the world by TripAdvisor Travelers' Choice in 2023.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB national park fee per person (foreigners), 40 THB (Thai nationals), children half price

topAttractions.name: Hat Chao Mai National Park
topAttractions.description: A 231 km² marine national park established in 1981, protecting a stretch of Trang's Andaman coastline plus the offshore islands of Koh Mook, Koh Kradan, Koh Cheuk, and parts of Koh Ngai. The park headquarters is at Hat Chao Mai beach (Hat Pak Meng area). It hosts mangrove forests, coral reefs, seagrass beds, and is an IUCN Category II ASEAN Heritage Park. Dugong sightings are possible from the park's boat routes.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults (foreigners), 100 THB children. Covers park entry and most island beaches within the park

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Hat Chao Mai National Park

topAttractions.name: Koh Libong Dugong Conservation Zone
topAttractions.description: Trang's largest island (approximately 40 km²) and Thailand's premier dugong-watching destination. Over 12,000 rai of seagrass meadows surrounding the island support a population of around 200 dugongs — more than half of Thailand's entire dugong population. Koh Libong also hosts significant populations of migratory birds at the Mu Ko Libong Non-Hunting Area. Longtail boat tours from the island's Ban Khuang Pier offer dugong-spotting trips at dawn and dusk.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free to enter island. Dugong boat tours: approx. 800–1,200 THB per longtail boat (shared among group)

topAttractions.name: Koh Ngai (Koh Hai)
topAttractions.description: A quiet, almost entirely resort-occupied island administratively in Trang Province but often marketed alongside Krabi's Koh Lanta (a short speedboat ride away). Koh Ngai offers arguably the best snorkeling in the Trang archipelago — a long shallow reef runs the length of the east coast with excellent hard and soft coral. There are no roads or cars on the island. Day-trippers arrive from Pak Meng Pier; overnight guests stay at one of a dozen small eco-resorts.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB national park fee per person (Hat Chao Mai NP). Snorkel gear rental at resorts: 200 THB/day

topAttractions.name: Trang City Morning Market & Dim Sum Street
topAttractions.description: Trang town's morning food scene is a cultural institution. From 06:00–10:00, open-air coffee shops and dim sum houses serve steamed buns, rice noodle rolls, siu mai, and the legendary moo yang (Trang roast pork) — a Hokkien Chinese tradition maintained for over a century. The area around Rassada Road and the clock tower is ground zero. Ruan Thai Dim Sum is the most famous restaurant. Entrance is free; budget 60–120 THB for a full breakfast.

sources.sourceName: Austin Bush Photography — Breakfast in Trang

localFood: Moo Yang (Trang Roast Pork) — whole pig marinated 8 hours in Hokkien Chinese spices, roasted until the skin is crisp and nearly charred; Trang's most iconic dish, sold as boxed souvenirs
Dim Sum — steamed buns, rice noodle rolls, and siu mai served at open-air coffee shops from 06:00; a Hokkien Chinese breakfast tradition unique to Trang
Khanom Jeen Nam Ya — fresh rice vermicelli with a thick, deeply spiced fish-based curry sauce; a southern Thai staple eaten morning to midday
Pla Too Tod (Fried Short Mackerel) — whole mackerel deep-fried until golden, served with rice and nam prik (chili paste); Trang's most affordable and ubiquitous lunch
Hoy Malaeng Phu (Mussels) — Trang's coastline produces excellent green-lipped mussels; eaten steamed with lemongrass or in a clear Thai herb broth
Roti (Southern-style flatbread) — Malay-influenced crispy pan-fried flatbread eaten with massaman or red curry, or as a sweet snack with condensed milk
Oysters from Kantang — Kantang District's brackish estuary produces prized oysters; eaten fresh with lime and chili, or grilled with oyster sauce at pier-side restaurants
Kaeng Tai Pla — intensely funky and spicy southern Thai curry made with fermented fish innards, bamboo shoots, and vegetables; not for the faint-hearted
culture: Trang's cultural identity is shaped by three overlapping influences: Theravada Buddhist Thai, Malay Muslim (concentrated in the southern districts bordering Satun), and a dominant Hokkien Chinese mercantile heritage that arrived with tin and rubber traders in the 19th century. The most tangible expression of this Chinese legacy is the morning dim sum and moo yang culture — practiced daily and treated as a communal ritual rather than mere sustenance. Trang was also the birthplace of Pridi Phanomyong, the Thai statesman who led the 1932 constitutional revolution. The Vegetarian Festival (Nine Emperor Gods Festival) in October sees the town transform with processions, firecrackers, and elaborate street shrines. Trang's annual Underwater Wedding Ceremony on Koh Kradan (Valentine's Day) is a quirky modern tradition that draws international media coverage.
gettingThere: Trang Airport (TST) lies 7 km south of the city center and is served by daily flights from Bangkok's Suvarnabhumi (BKK) on Nok Air and AirAsia (approx. 90 min, from 1,500 THB one-way). Minibuses to the city center cost 90 THB. By train from Bangkok, the overnight Express #83 (departs 18:50, arrives 08:30) and Rapid #167 (departs 20:30, arrives 11:00) both offer 2nd-class air-con sleepers and pull into Trang Railway Station in the city center. By bus, Southern Bangkok Terminal (Sai Tai Mai) has seven daily departures from 16:30–18:00 (500–600 THB, 14 hours). From Hat Yai, minibuses run hourly (100 THB, 3 hours). From Krabi, minibuses and shared taxis take approximately 2 hours.
gettingAround: Trang city is compact and walkable for the market and food areas. Shared songthaews (pickup truck taxis) connect the city to the island ferry piers at Pak Meng (45 min, 60 THB) and Hat Yao/Kuan Thung Khu (30–40 min, 60–80 THB), departing when full from the Trang town terminal from 07:00–16:00. Longtail boats and speedboats connect the piers to Koh Libong (30 min, 60 THB), Koh Mook (45 min), Koh Kradan (1 hr), and Koh Ngai (1 hr). Motorcycle taxis and tuk-tuks handle short town trips (30–60 THB). Renting a motorbike (250–350 THB/day) is the best way to explore inland districts.
travelTips: The Emerald Cave can only be visited November through April — check sea conditions before booking any island tour
Trang's dim sum breakfast is best experienced by 07:30 AM — most shops sell out of moo yang by 09:00
Pak Meng Pier is the main departure point for Koh Ngai and Koh Mook; Hat Yao Pier serves Koh Libong and Koh Kradan — confirm which pier your tour departs from
Koh Libong dugong tours are most productive at dawn (06:00–08:00) and late afternoon — book the previous evening with local fishing village guides, not tour agencies
Trang town has excellent ATMs and pharmacies but the islands have almost no facilities — bring enough cash and any medications before boarding a ferry
The train journey from Bangkok to Trang is scenic and comfortable — book 2nd-class sleepers at least 3 days in advance, especially for Friday and Sunday night departures
Hat Chao Mai National Park fees (200 THB) are charged at island landings, not at the mainland piers — carry exact cash
Trang is far less crowded than Krabi or Koh Samui year-round — this is a genuine advantage, not a warning
